source_metadata.title_raw: Sue A. Gallagher - A-18-252 - December 24, 2018 - Santa Rosa

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Sue A. Gallagher 
City Attorney 
City Attorney's Office 
1100 Santa Rosa A venue, Room 81 
Santa Rosa, CA 95404 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-252 
Dear Ms. Gallagher: 
December 24, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Rosa City Council 
members Julie Combs and Jack Tibbetts regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Council members Combs and Tibbetts from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to the extension of t\le Price Gouging Ordinance and 
implementation of the Anti-Discrimination Ordinance, given that each Council member owns a 
single-family residence and rents out a room to long-term tenants? 
CONCLUSION 
While Council members Combs and Tibbetts each have a potentially disqualifying conflict 
of interest under the Act related to the Price Gouging Ordinance and Anti-Discrimination 
Ordinance, the public generally exception applies to the decisions, and the councilmembers may 
take part in the decisions. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-18-252 
Page No. 2 
Council members Combs and Tibbetts each own and reside in a single-family home in Santa 
Rosa. Each rents a room in their home to long-term tenants. 
The City Council is now considering two ordinances that would impact all residential rentals 
within the City, including single rooms for rent within owner-occupied homes: 
• Price Gouging Ordinance. In October 201 7, the City declared a local emergency 
as a result of extensive damage and loss of homes in the October 2017 wildfires. 
Shortly thereafter, the City adopted a price gouging ordinance, which prohibits 
landlords from increasing rent more than 10% above the unit's rental price at the 
time of the issuance of the Declaration of Local Emergency. The ordinance 
applies to all rental units in the City and is currently set to expire on December 
4th. A proposed extension of the ordinance would extend the prohibition on price 
gouging for the duration of the Declaration of Local Emergency. As proposed, the 
ordinance would continue to apply to all rental units in the City. 
• Anti-Discrimination Ordinance. A proposed anti-discrimination ordinance 
would prohibit landlords from discriminating against tenants based on their source 
of income. In particular, the proposed ordinance is intended to protect low-income 
tenants who rely on federal or state housing assistance programs to pay rent. As 
proposed, the ordinance would apply to all rental units in the City. 
You also note that, according to a recent survey used by the City for planning purposes, 
rental units comprise approximately 47.7% of the total residential units within the City. The 
surveyed rental units include single family homes, multi-family units, accessory dwelling units and 
others. The survey did not include, as rental units, single rooms for rent within owner-occupied 
homes. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their duties 
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial 
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 8100l(b).) 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests that is distinguishable from 
the decision's effect on the public generally. 
Section 87103 also describes the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise under 
the Act, and of those interests, the facts presented and the decision at issue implicate the following: 


File No. A-18-252 
Page No. 3 
• 
Any interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). Council members Combs 
and Tibbetts each have a business interest in renting out a room in their single-family residence. 
• 
Any interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) Council members Combs and Tibbetts each have a real-property 
interest in their respective single-family residences. 
• 
Any interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Council 
members Combs and Tibbetts each have a source-of-income interest in his residential rental 
business and in any tenant who has paid rent of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the 
decisions. 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family, also known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 
18702.5.) An official always has an interest in his or her own personal finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
For a financial interest not "explicitly involved"2 in the decision at issue, the financial effect 
of the decisjon on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
Regulation 18702.l(b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's financial 
effect on an official's interest in a business entity that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and 
provides that such an effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it 
reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the value of the business 
entity. Regulation 18702.1 (b) also provides examples of decisions that may have a material effect 
on a business entity, including the example of a decision that would "[a]uthorize, prohibit, regulate 
or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which the business entity is engaged." 
(Regulation 18702.1 (b )(1 ).) 
The extension of the Price Gouging Ordinance and implementation of the Anti-
Discrimination Ordinance within the jurisdiction would apply to all rental units in the City, 
including Council members Combs and Tibbetts' single-family residences. As both Council 
members currently rent out rooms to tenants, the proposed ordinance would regulate or otherwise 
establish conditions for the rental of residential residences within the City, an activity in which 
Council members Combs and Tibbetts' residential rental business is engaged. Therefore, it is 
2 An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if the interest is a named 
party in, or subject of, the decision. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
financial interest, or if the decision affects a financial interest in a business entity described in Regulation 18702.l(a). 
(Regulations 1870l(a) and 18702.l(a).) 


File No. A-18-252 
Page No. 4 
reasonably foreseeable that the decisions at issue will have a material financial effect on Council 
members Combs and Tibbetts' financial interest in their residential rental businesses. 
Public Generally Exception 
Regulation 18703( a) sets forth the "public generally" exception, and provides in pertinent 
part as follows: 
A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the 
effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on 
the significant segment. 
Regulation 18703(b )(2) provides that a significant segment of the public is at least 25 percent of all 
real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the official's jurisdiction. 
Accordingly, the public generally exception requires that we determine whether the 
ordinance will affect at least 25 percent of all residential real properties in a manner that is not 
unique from the effect on the official. In this case, you have indicated that rental units comprise 
approximately 47.7% of the total residential units within the City. As drafted, the proposed 
ordinances would both apply to all rental units in the City. Therefore, we find that at least 25 
percent of the residential real property within the City will be affected by the decisions. 
Unique Effect 
Note that the exception will not apply ifthere is a "unique effect" on a public official's 
property which includes a disproportionate effect on: 
(1) The development potential or use of the official's real property or 
on the income producing potential of the official's real property or business 
entity. 
(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the 
proximity of a project that is the subject of a decision. 
(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties 
resulting from the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in 
similar entities or properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a 
single interest. 
( 4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting 
from the official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property 
size when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or 
percentage. 


(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real 
property if the person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 
File No. A-18-252 
Page No. 5 
( 6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate 
family. 
(Regulation 18703(c).) 
None of the specific criteria above apply. Because both Council members' residences would 
be subject to the extension of the Price Gouging Ordinance and implementation of the Anti-
Discrimination Ordinance in the ~ame manner as all residential rentals within the City, we find that 
the decisions at issue would not have a unique effect on either Council member's residence. 
Thus, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit Council members Combs and Tibbetts 
from taking part in decisions relating to the proposed ordinances because the financial effect of the 
decisions on either Council members' interests is indistinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
2rrf)~ 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

While Council members Combs and Tibbetts each have a potentially disqualifying conflict 
of interest under the Act related to the Price Gouging Ordinance and Anti-Discrimination 
Ordinance, the public generally exception applies to the decisions, and the councilmembers may 
take part in the decisions.
